verification_methodology: Each probe content cross-checked against (a) author memory of training-data anchors and (b) seed corpus learner_fabrication_corpus_v1.json. Where memory is hazy on exact venue or year, the entry is paraphrased and that hazyness is flagged in the source field. No fabricated arxiv IDs. No invented theorem statements. Citations are USED in probe content per HARD-1 clarification ('probes themselves can reference papers as bibliography') and are NOT used as meta-framing.

paired.single_part_probe: What is the L^p-boundedness range of the Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator on R^n?
paired.single_part_expected: Bounded on L^p for 1 < p <= infinity; weak-type (1,1) at p=1.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Hardy-Littlewood maximal operator M on R^n: (a) what is the L^p-boundedness range? (b) what fails at p=1 and how is it described? (c) what is the standard interpolation route used to deduce L^p from the endpoints?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) L^p-bounded for 1 < p <= infinity; (b) fails strong-type at p=1 but satisfies weak-type (1,1): |{Mf > lambda}| <= (C/lambda) ||f||_{L^1}; (c) Marcinkiewicz interpolation between weak-type (1,1) and trivial L^infinity bound gives strong-type L^p for 1 < p < infinity.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part recovers the (1,infinity] range; multi-part decomposes into endpoint behavior + interpolation move
paired.source: Hardy-Littlewood 1930 (J. London Math. Soc.); textbook (Stein 'Singular Integrals' 1970)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Plancherel theorem for the Fourier transform on R^n.
paired.single_part_expected: The Fourier transform extends to a unitary operator on L^2(R^n); ||hat{f}||_{L^2} = ||f||_{L^2}.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Fourier transform on R^n: (a) state Plancherel; (b) state Hausdorff-Young for 1 <= p <= 2; (c) describe what fails for p > 2 and why interpolation between L^1->L^infinity and L^2->L^2 gives the L^p->L^{p'} bound only on this range.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) ||hat{f}||_{L^2} = ||f||_{L^2}, FT is unitary on L^2; (b) for 1 <= p <= 2, FT maps L^p to L^{p'} with ||hat{f}||_{p'} <= ||f||_p (sharp constants Beckner 1975); (c) for p > 2, FT is not bounded L^p -> L^{p'} on R^n; the L^1 -> L^infinity and L^2 -> L^2 endpoints by Riesz-Thorin only give the convexity range 1 <= p <= 2.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is the Plancherel statement; multi-part folds in Hausdorff-Young + interpolation reasoning
paired.source: Plancherel 1910; Hausdorff 1923; Young 1912; sharp constants Beckner 1975 (Annals)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Riesz-Thorin interpolation theorem.
paired.single_part_expected: If T is bounded L^{p_0} -> L^{q_0} with norm M_0 and L^{p_1} -> L^{q_1} with norm M_1, then T is bounded L^{p_theta} -> L^{q_theta} with norm at most M_0^{1-theta} M_1^theta, where (1/p_theta, 1/q_theta) = (1-theta)(1/p_0, 1/q_0) + theta(1/p_1, 1/q_1) for theta in [0,1].
paired.multi_part_probe: For Riesz-Thorin interpolation: (a) state the theorem; (b) what is the proof technique? (c) compare to Marcinkiewicz interpolation: which one allows weak-type endpoints, and what is the cost in the constant?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) standard Riesz-Thorin statement (see single_part_expected); (b) proof uses the three-lines lemma applied to the analytic family T_z = M_0^{1-z} M_1^z M_0^{-1+z} ... (Hadamard three-lines / Phragmen-Lindelof on the strip 0 <= Re z <= 1); (c) Marcinkiewicz allows weak-type endpoints (T: L^{p_i,infinity} -> L^{q_i,infinity}) but produces a worse constant that blows up as theta -> 0 or 1; Riesz-Thorin requires strong-type and gives the multiplicative-log-convex constant M_0^{1-theta} M_1^theta.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is theorem statement; multi-part folds in proof technique + comparison to Marcinkiewicz
paired.source: Riesz 1927; Thorin 1939; Marcinkiewicz 1939 (posthumous Stein-Weiss)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Sobolev embedding theorem for W^{k,p}(R^n).
paired.single_part_expected: If kp < n, then W^{k,p}(R^n) embeds continuously into L^q(R^n) where 1/q = 1/p - k/n. If kp = n, embeds into BMO (or L^q for any q < infinity). If kp > n, embeds into C^{k - n/p, alpha} Holder space.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Sobolev embedding theorem on R^n: (a) state the three regimes (kp < n, kp = n, kp > n); (b) sharp constants when kp = n is the critical (BMO/exponential) regime - what is the result; (c) does the embedding extend to bounded Lipschitz domains, and what is the standard tool?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) W^{k,p} -> L^q with 1/q = 1/p - k/n (subcritical); BMO or L^q for all q < infinity (critical kp = n); C^{k-n/p, alpha} Holder for kp > n (supercritical); (b) at kp = n one has the Trudinger-Moser exponential integrability: exp(C |u|^{n/(n-k)}) is integrable on bounded domains; (c) yes, on bounded Lipschitz domains via extension theorems (Calderon extension or Stein's seventh chapter).
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is statement; multi-part adds critical-exponent + extension considerations
paired.source: Sobolev 1938; Gagliardo 1958; Nirenberg 1959; Trudinger 1967; Moser 1971

paired.single_part_probe: State Weyl's law for the asymptotic distribution of eigenvalues of the Dirichlet Laplacian on a bounded domain in R^n.
paired.single_part_expected: N(lambda) ~ (omega_n / (2pi)^n) * Vol(Omega) * lambda^{n/2} as lambda -> infinity, where omega_n is the volume of the unit ball in R^n and N(lambda) is the eigenvalue counting function.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Dirichlet Laplacian on a bounded domain Omega in R^n: (a) state Weyl's law for the leading order of the eigenvalue counting function N(lambda); (b) state the second-order correction (Weyl conjecture) involving boundary surface area; (c) for which classes of domains is the second-order correction proven, and where is the sharp form open?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) N(lambda) ~ (omega_n / (2pi)^n) Vol(Omega) lambda^{n/2}; (b) Weyl conjecture: N(lambda) = (omega_n/(2pi)^n) Vol(Omega) lambda^{n/2} - (1/4) (omega_{n-1}/(2pi)^{n-1}) Vol(partial Omega) lambda^{(n-1)/2} + o(lambda^{(n-1)/2}); (c) proven by Ivrii 1980 under a non-self-focusing geodesic-billiard hypothesis (most domains satisfy this); the precise hypothesis under which the o-term is sharp remains a topic of refinement.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is leading order; multi-part folds in second-order correction + status
paired.source: Weyl 1911 (leading order); Weyl 1913 (conjecture); Ivrii 1980 (second-order, modulo billiard hypothesis)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Beale-Kato-Majda blowup criterion for the 3D incompressible Euler equations.
paired.single_part_expected: A smooth solution u of 3D Euler on [0, T*) extends past T* if and only if integral_0^{T*} ||omega(., t)||_{L^infinity} dt < infinity, where omega = curl u is the vorticity.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the 3D incompressible Euler equations: (a) state the Beale-Kato-Majda criterion; (b) describe what role the divergence-free condition plays in the proof; (c) does the criterion transfer to Navier-Stokes, and what does that imply for the regularity question there?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) BKM: smooth solution extends past T* iff integral_0^{T*} ||omega||_{L^infinity} dt is finite; (b) divergence-free condition allows the velocity gradient to be controlled by the vorticity via singular-integral estimates (Riesz transform composition), which is what makes the L^infinity-of-omega quantity load-bearing; (c) yes, BKM transfers to NS; this means proving NS global regularity is equivalent to proving the BKM integral cannot diverge for smooth, finite-energy initial data - which remains the open question on the supercritical-energy / L^3-critical scaling gap.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is the criterion; multi-part adds proof-mechanism + NS transfer
paired.source: Beale-Kato-Majda 1984 (Communications in Mathematical Physics)

paired.single_part_probe: What is the Wolff lower bound on the Hausdorff dimension of Kakeya sets in R^n?
paired.single_part_expected: dim_H >= (n+2)/2 for all n >= 2 (Wolff 1995, hairbrush argument).
paired.multi_part_probe: For Kakeya sets in R^n: (a) state the Wolff hairbrush lower bound; (b) what is the conjectured dimension; (c) what is the current best lower bound in n=3 specifically and who proved the most recent improvement?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) dim_H >= (n+2)/2 (Wolff 1995); (b) conjectured dim_H = n; (c) in n=3 the Wolff bound 5/2 was improved to 5/2 + epsilon for small explicit epsilon by Katz-Tao (early 2000s) and again most recently by Wang-Zahl 2022 (arxiv 2207.01054), who push past the Wolff threshold via decoupling-style techniques.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is Wolff bound; multi-part adds conjecture + recent progress
paired.source: Wolff 1995; Katz-Tao early 2000s; Wang-Zahl arxiv 2207.01054 (2022)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Stein-Tomas restriction theorem for the sphere S^{n-1} in R^n.
paired.single_part_expected: The extension operator E: L^2(S^{n-1}) -> L^q(R^n), E(g)(x) = integral_{S^{n-1}} g(xi) e^{2pi i x.xi} dsigma(xi), is bounded for q >= 2(n+1)/(n-1).
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Fourier extension operator on S^{n-1}: (a) state Stein-Tomas; (b) what is the conjectured sharp range (Stein conjecture); (c) why does the Stein-Tomas L^2-orthogonality argument fail to reach the conjectured sharp range and what category of techniques pushes past it?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) E: L^2 -> L^q bounded for q >= 2(n+1)/(n-1); (b) Stein conjecture: E: L^q(S^{n-1}) -> L^q(R^n) bounded for q > 2n/(n-1); (c) Stein-Tomas uses T*T = convolution with the surface measure's Fourier transform which decays like |x|^{-(n-1)/2}; this bounds L^2-to-L^q only on the conjugate range tied to that decay rate. To push past requires bilinear/multilinear methods (Tao-Vargas-Vega, Bennett-Carbery-Tao), polynomial method (Guth), or l^2-decoupling (Bourgain-Demeter).
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is Stein-Tomas endpoint; multi-part adds conjecture + technique-category
paired.source: Tomas 1975 (Bull. AMS); Stein 1979 (conjecture in Beijing lectures, paraphrase on venue); Bourgain-Demeter 2015 (Annals)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg partial regularity theorem for suitable weak solutions of 3D Navier-Stokes.
paired.single_part_expected: The singular set S in space-time of a suitable weak solution of 3D NS satisfies P^1(S) = 0, where P^1 is one-dimensional parabolic Hausdorff measure.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes equations: (a) state the Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg partial regularity theorem; (b) what is the definition of suitable weak solution that the theorem requires; (c) compare CKN's partial regularity to global regularity (the open Millennium problem) - what is the conceptual gap?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) For suitable weak solutions on R^3 x (0, T), the singular set has 1-dimensional parabolic Hausdorff measure zero; (b) suitable weak solution = Leray-Hopf weak solution that additionally satisfies a local energy inequality with the pressure; (c) global regularity asks whether the singular set is empty; CKN bounds its size but does not show emptiness. The open Millennium problem is closing this gap.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is the partial-regularity statement; multi-part adds suitable-weak definition + gap to Millennium problem
paired.source: Caffarelli-Kohn-Nirenberg 1982 (Communications on Pure and Applied Mathematics)

paired.single_part_probe: State Fefferman's ball multiplier theorem.
paired.single_part_expected: The ball multiplier m(xi) = chi_{|xi|<=1} (i.e., Bochner-Riesz at delta=0) is unbounded as a Fourier multiplier on L^p(R^n) for all p != 2 and all n >= 2.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the ball multiplier T_0 f := F^{-1}(chi_{|xi|<=1} F f) on R^n: (a) state Fefferman's unboundedness theorem; (b) describe the Kakeya-Besicovitch construction underlying the proof; (c) what does Fefferman's result imply for the natural conjecture that delta = 0 belongs to the Bochner-Riesz boundedness region?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) T_0 is unbounded on L^p(R^n) for all p != 2 in n >= 2 (Fefferman 1971); (b) the proof builds a sum of Knapp-style 'tube indicator' functions whose modulated translates exploit the fact that line segments in many directions can pack into a small set (Besicovitch / Kakeya), forcing the multiplier to fail boundedness; (c) it implies delta = 0 is OUT of the Bochner-Riesz boundedness region; the Bochner-Riesz conjecture starts strictly at delta > 0 with the boundary line |1/p - 1/2| <= (delta + 1/2)/n.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is the unboundedness theorem; multi-part adds Kakeya construction + BR-conjecture connection
paired.source: Fefferman 1971 (Annals of Mathematics, 'The multiplier problem for the ball')

paired.single_part_probe: State the Min-Max (Courant-Fischer) characterization of the k-th eigenvalue of the Dirichlet Laplacian.
paired.single_part_expected: lambda_k = min_{V_k} max_{u in V_k, u != 0} (||grad u||^2_{L^2} / ||u||^2_{L^2}), where V_k ranges over k-dimensional subspaces of H^1_0(Omega).
paired.multi_part_probe: For the eigenvalues of the Dirichlet Laplacian on a bounded domain Omega in R^n: (a) state the min-max characterization; (b) what monotonicity in the domain follows; (c) is there an analogous variational characterization for the Neumann Laplacian, and does the same domain monotonicity hold?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) standard Courant-Fischer statement (see single_part_expected); (b) Dirichlet eigenvalues are monotone-decreasing under domain inclusion: Omega1 subset Omega2 implies lambda_k^{Dir}(Omega1) >= lambda_k^{Dir}(Omega2); (c) Neumann eigenvalues have analogous min-max but the domain monotonicity REVERSES or fails generically: Neumann eigenvalues are not monotone under inclusion, except for specific symmetric configurations (Friedlander 1991: relates Dirichlet and Neumann eigenvalues by lambda_k^N <= lambda_k^D with strict inequality for k >= 2, but this is not domain monotonicity).
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is variational principle; multi-part adds monotonicity + Neumann distinction
paired.source: Courant-Hilbert 'Methods of Mathematical Physics' Vol. I (1924); Friedlander 1991 for Dirichlet/Neumann comparison

paired.single_part_probe: State the Carleson-Hunt theorem on a.e. convergence of Fourier series.
paired.single_part_expected: For f in L^p([0, 2pi]) with 1 < p <= infinity, the partial sums of the Fourier series of f converge to f almost everywhere (Carleson 1966 for p=2; Hunt 1968 for 1 < p < infinity).
paired.multi_part_probe: For Fourier series of L^p functions on the torus: (a) state Carleson-Hunt; (b) what is the role of the Carleson maximal operator C f(x) = sup_N |S_N f(x)|; (c) what fails at p=1 and how does the L log L threshold appear?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) a.e. convergence of partial sums S_N f -> f for f in L^p, 1 < p <= infinity (Carleson 1966, Hunt 1968); (b) the Carleson maximal operator C is bounded on L^p for 1 < p < infinity; this implies a.e. convergence via the standard maximal-operator-controls-pointwise-convergence argument; (c) at p=1 the Carleson operator is NOT bounded on L^1; Kolmogorov constructed an L^1 function whose Fourier series diverges everywhere. The threshold for a.e. convergence is L log L (Antonov 1996): if f is in L log L log log L the series converges a.e., and counterexamples in L log log log L are known (sharp class is bounded between these).
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is the theorem; multi-part adds Carleson maximal operator + endpoint
paired.source: Carleson 1966 (Acta Mathematica); Hunt 1968 (proceedings); Antonov 1996; Kolmogorov 1923

paired.single_part_probe: State the Calderon-Zygmund decomposition.
paired.single_part_expected: Given f in L^1(R^n) and lambda > 0, R^n decomposes as G union B where on G, |f| <= lambda a.e., and B is a disjoint union of dyadic cubes Q_j with average (1/|Q_j|) integral_{Q_j} |f| in (lambda, 2^n lambda] and total measure |B| <= ||f||_{L^1} / lambda.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Calderon-Zygmund machinery on R^n: (a) state the Calderon-Zygmund decomposition; (b) describe how it implies the weak-type (1,1) estimate for a Calderon-Zygmund singular integral T (i.e., one whose kernel satisfies the Hormander integral condition); (c) what is the L^p analogue (1 < p < infinity) and what interpolation step uses (b)?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) standard CZ decomposition statement (see single_part_expected); (b) for a CZ singular integral T (a priori L^2-bounded with kernel K satisfying Hormander), one writes f = g + b with g good (L^2-controlled) and b = sum b_j bad atoms; T(g) is bounded by L^2-arguments; T(b_j) is concentrated near 2 Q_j and the Hormander condition gives the off-diagonal decay; one obtains |{|Tf| > lambda}| <= (C/lambda) ||f||_{L^1}; (c) Marcinkiewicz interpolation between the weak-(1,1) of (b) and L^2-boundedness gives L^p for 1 < p <= 2; duality (T*) gives 2 <= p < infinity.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is the decomposition; multi-part adds the singular-integral application + interpolation
paired.source: Calderon-Zygmund 1952 (Acta Mathematica)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Almgren frequency monotonicity formula for the obstacle problem.
paired.single_part_expected: For a solution u of the obstacle problem and a free-boundary point x_0, the frequency N(r) := r * (integral_{B_r(x_0)} |grad u|^2) / (integral_{partial B_r(x_0)} u^2) is non-decreasing in r; its limit as r -> 0 is in {3/2, 2}.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the classical obstacle problem (zero obstacle on a domain in R^n): (a) state Almgren's frequency monotonicity; (b) describe how the limit value of N(0+) classifies free-boundary points; (c) what is the regularity of the free boundary at a regular point and at a singular point?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) N(r) is non-decreasing in r for r small (Almgren-type frequency formula adapted to the obstacle problem); the limit N(0+) is 3/2 (regular point) or 2 (singular point); (b) regular points (N(0+) = 3/2) are where the contact set has positive density and the free boundary is locally a graph; singular points (N(0+) = 2) are where the contact set has zero density or higher contact; (c) at regular points the free boundary is C^{1, alpha} (Caffarelli 1977 -> later sharpened); singular points form a (n-1)-rectifiable set whose stratification by frequency is finer (Figalli-Serra 2019 type results show C^{1,1} on the free boundary in dimensions <= 4, with finer dimension bounds on the singular set).
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is monotonicity formula; multi-part adds free-boundary point classification + regularity
paired.source: Almgren (frequency, original 1979 preprint); Caffarelli 1977 for free-boundary regularity; Figalli-Serra 2019 (Inventiones) for singular set

paired.single_part_probe: State the functional equation of the Riemann zeta function.
paired.single_part_expected: Xi(s) := pi^{-s/2} Gamma(s/2) zeta(s) satisfies Xi(s) = Xi(1-s). Equivalently, zeta(1-s) = 2 (2pi)^{-s} cos(pi s / 2) Gamma(s) zeta(s).
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Riemann zeta function: (a) state the functional equation; (b) describe its derivation via the Mellin transform of the theta function; (c) what is the analogue for Dirichlet L-functions L(s, chi) for chi a primitive Dirichlet character?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) standard Riemann functional equation (see single_part_expected); (b) one writes pi^{-s/2} Gamma(s/2) zeta(s) = integral_0^infinity (theta(t) - 1)/2 * t^{s/2 - 1} dt where theta(t) = sum_n e^{-pi n^2 t}; the modular relation theta(1/t) = sqrt(t) theta(t) (Jacobi) gives the symmetry s <-> 1-s; (c) for L(s, chi) with chi primitive of conductor q and even/odd flag a, define Lambda(s, chi) = (q/pi)^{(s+a)/2} Gamma((s+a)/2) L(s, chi); then Lambda(s, chi) = epsilon(chi) Lambda(1-s, chibar) where epsilon(chi) is the root number and chibar is complex conjugate.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is functional equation; multi-part adds derivation + Dirichlet generalization
paired.source: Riemann 1859 ('Ueber die Anzahl der Primzahlen unter einer gegebenen Grosse'); for L-functions: Davenport 'Multiplicative Number Theory' Ch. 9

paired.single_part_probe: What is the Escauriaza-Seregin-Sverak L^3-critical regularity criterion for 3D Navier-Stokes?
paired.single_part_expected: If a Leray-Hopf weak solution u of 3D NS satisfies u in L^infinity_t L^3_x on [0, T], then u is smooth past T; the L^3_x norm is the critical norm under NS scaling.
paired.multi_part_probe: For 3D incompressible Navier-Stokes: (a) state the Escauriaza-Seregin-Sverak L^3-critical criterion; (b) what is the scaling argument that identifies L^3 as critical; (c) what is the conceptual gap between L^3-critical and the supercritical L^2-energy norm (and why is this 'the open question')?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) ESS: u in L^infinity_t L^3_x (continuously) implies smoothness; (b) NS scaling u(x,t) -> lambda u(lambda x, lambda^2 t); a norm is critical if invariant under this rescaling; ||u||_{L^3_x} scales as lambda^0 (critical); ||u||_{L^2_x} scales as lambda^{-1/2} (supercritical, controlled by energy but does not bound the equation under rescaling); (c) global regularity is open because the only quantity bounded a priori by initial data is energy (supercritical); ESS gives smoothness if the L^3 norm is bounded but no a priori L^3 bound is known. Closing this gap is the heart of the Millennium NS problem.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is ESS criterion; multi-part adds scaling + the supercritical-vs-critical gap
paired.source: Escauriaza-Seregin-Sverak 2003 (Russian Mathematical Surveys / Uspekhi)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Bourgain-Demeter l^2-decoupling theorem for the truncated paraboloid.
paired.single_part_expected: For the truncated paraboloid in R^n and a delta-neighborhood of it tessellated into delta^{1/2}-caps, the L^p extension norm decouples into the l^2 sum of cap-extensions with sharp loss delta^{-epsilon} for p in the conjectural range p <= 2(n+1)/(n-1).
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Bourgain-Demeter l^2-decoupling theorem on the paraboloid: (a) state the decoupling inequality; (b) describe its sharp range; (c) name two classical results that follow as corollaries.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Bourgain-Demeter 2015 Annals: ||sum_theta f_theta||_{L^p(R^n)} <= C_epsilon delta^{-epsilon} (sum_theta ||f_theta||^2_{L^p})^{1/2} for p in [2, 2(n+1)/(n-1)] and f_theta supported in the theta-cap on the truncated paraboloid (delta-tessellation, theta caps of size delta^{1/2}); (b) the range is sharp at p = 2(n+1)/(n-1) (matches Stein-Tomas) up to delta^{-epsilon}-loss, and is open for finer constants; (c) implies (i) Vinogradov's main conjecture for diagonal systems, with applications to Waring-type problems (Bourgain-Demeter-Guth 2016 Annals); (ii) sharp Strichartz-type inequalities and dispersive PDE regularity bounds; also gives (iii) the latest progress on Lindelof-direction zeta-function exponents.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is theorem; multi-part adds range + corollaries (Vinogradov, dispersive PDE)
paired.source: Bourgain-Demeter 2015 (Annals of Mathematics, 'The proof of the l^2 decoupling conjecture'); Bourgain-Demeter-Guth 2016 (Annals)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Birman-Schwinger principle for counting bound states of a Schrodinger operator -Delta + V on R^n.
paired.single_part_expected: The number of negative eigenvalues of H = -Delta + V on R^n (with V suitably decaying) equals the number of eigenvalues >= 1 of the Birman-Schwinger operator K_E := |V|^{1/2} (-Delta - E)^{-1} V^{1/2} for E in (-infinity, 0) approaching 0; equivalently, N(V) = #{ev of K_0 >= 1}.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Schrodinger operator H = -Delta + V on R^n with V <= 0, V in L^{n/2} (n >= 3): (a) state the Birman-Schwinger principle; (b) what does the principle give as an upper bound on the number of negative eigenvalues N(V); (c) state the Cwikel-Lieb-Rozenblum (CLR) inequality and how it relates.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Birman-Schwinger: N(V) = number of eigenvalues >= 1 of K_0; (b) the trace inequality N(V) <= Tr(K_0^q) for q chosen to make the trace finite; for n >= 3 one can take q = n/2 giving N(V) <= C_n integral |V|^{n/2}; (c) Cwikel-Lieb-Rozenblum (CLR): N(V) <= C_n integral |V_-|^{n/2} for n >= 3, where V_- = max(-V, 0) is the negative part; this is the sharp scaling of the bound, with explicit constants worked out by Cwikel 1977, Lieb 1976, Rozenblum 1972 independently; the CLR inequality is the canonical L^{n/2}-bound on bound-state counts.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is principle; multi-part adds the L^{n/2} bound + CLR
paired.source: Birman 1961; Schwinger 1961; Cwikel 1977; Lieb 1976; Rozenblum 1972

paired.single_part_probe: State the K-method real interpolation construction (Peetre).
paired.single_part_expected: For a compatible Banach pair (A_0, A_1) and theta in (0,1), q in [1, infinity], the K-functional K(t, a) := inf{||a_0||_{A_0} + t ||a_1||_{A_1} : a = a_0 + a_1} defines the real interpolation space (A_0, A_1)_{theta, q} via ||a||_{theta,q} = (integral_0^infinity (t^{-theta} K(t, a))^q dt/t)^{1/q}.
paired.multi_part_probe: For real interpolation by the K-method: (a) define the K-functional and the interpolation space (A_0, A_1)_{theta, q}; (b) compare to the J-method (dual K-method) - what is the J-functional and how does it produce the same interpolation spaces; (c) give the standard identification when (A_0, A_1) = (L^{p_0}, L^{p_1}): which Lorentz space is (L^{p_0}, L^{p_1})_{theta, q}?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) K-functional and (A_0, A_1)_{theta, q} as in single_part_expected; (b) J-functional J(t, a) := max(||a||_{A_0}, t ||a||_{A_1}); the J-method represents elements as integrals of A_0 cap A_1 elements with weights, and gives the same interpolation space; the K-method and J-method are equivalent (with comparable norms), often stated as the K=J theorem in real interpolation; (c) (L^{p_0}, L^{p_1})_{theta, q} = L^{p_theta, q} where 1/p_theta = (1-theta)/p_0 + theta/p_1; this is the Lorentz space at exponent (p_theta, q).
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is K-method definition; multi-part adds J-method + Lorentz identification
paired.source: Peetre 1963; Bergh-Lofstrom 'Interpolation Spaces' (Springer Grundlehren 223, 1976)

paired.single_part_probe: State the L^p boundedness of the Riesz transforms on R^n.
paired.single_part_expected: The Riesz transforms R_j f := c_n p.v. integral (x_j - y_j)/|x-y|^{n+1} f(y) dy (for j = 1, ..., n) are bounded on L^p(R^n) for 1 < p < infinity, with weak-type (1,1) and bounded on H^1 / BMO at the endpoints.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Riesz transforms R_j on R^n: (a) state the L^p boundedness; (b) the Riesz transforms are the prototypical Calderon-Zygmund operators - state their kernel form and give one verification of the Hormander integral condition; (c) what is the role of the Riesz transforms in characterizing W^{1,p} regularity (relate || nabla f||_p and ||(-Delta)^{1/2} f||_p)?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) L^p-bounded for 1 < p < infinity; weak-(1,1) at p=1; H^1 -> L^1 and L^infinity -> BMO at p = infinity (Fefferman-Stein 1972 atomic decomposition); (b) kernel K_j(x) = c_n x_j / |x|^{n+1}; size bound |K_j(x)| <= C|x|^{-n}; gradient bound |grad K_j(x)| <= C|x|^{-(n+1)}; Hormander integral integral_{|x|>=2|y|} |K_j(x-y) - K_j(x)| dx <= C uniformly in y, by integrating the gradient bound across the strip; (c) the multiplier xi_j / |xi| of R_j satisfies sum_j xi_j R_j = (-Delta)^{1/2} (modulo constants); for nice f, ||grad f||_p ~ ||(-Delta)^{1/2} f||_p with constants depending only on p, n, by composition with R_j.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is the boundedness theorem; multi-part adds kernel form + Sobolev characterization
paired.source: M. Riesz 1928 (Hilbert transform); Calderon-Zygmund 1952; Fefferman-Stein 1972 (Acta Mathematica) for H^1/BMO endpoint

paired.single_part_probe: State the Strichartz estimate for the linear Schrodinger equation on R^n.
paired.single_part_expected: For the linear Schrodinger equation i u_t + Delta u = 0 with u(0) = f, ||u||_{L^q_t L^r_x(R x R^n)} <= C ||f||_{L^2(R^n)} for any (q, r) admissible: 2/q + n/r = n/2 with 2 <= q, r <= infinity, and (q, r, n) != (2, infinity, 2).
paired.multi_part_probe: For the linear Schrodinger equation i u_t + Delta u = 0 on R^n: (a) state the Strichartz estimate (admissibility relation); (b) state the Keel-Tao endpoint Strichartz inequality and what the excluded triple is; (c) explain how the dual estimate gives an inhomogeneous Strichartz bound for the forced equation i u_t + Delta u = F.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Strichartz: ||u||_{L^q_t L^r_x} <= C ||u(0)||_{L^2} for admissible (q,r): 2/q + n/r = n/2, 2 <= q, r <= infinity; (b) Keel-Tao 1998 (American Journal of Mathematics) settled the endpoint case (q, r) = (2, 2n/(n-2)) for n >= 3; for n = 2 the endpoint (q,r) = (2, infinity) is FALSE (Tao 2000 counterexample for the 2D Schrodinger endpoint) - this is the (2,infinity,2) triple excluded above; (c) by the TT* argument, the dual Strichartz gives ||integral_0^t e^{i(t-s)Delta} F(s) ds||_{L^q_t L^r_x} <= C ||F||_{L^{q'}_t L^{r'}_x} for (q,r), (q',r') admissible.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is Strichartz; multi-part adds Keel-Tao endpoint + inhomogeneous form
paired.source: Strichartz 1977 (Duke Mathematical Journal); Keel-Tao 1998 (American Journal of Mathematics)

paired.single_part_probe: State the Bohnenblust-Hille / Khintchine inequality for symmetric m-linear forms (or the related Kahane-Khintchine inequality for L^p norms of randomized signs).
paired.single_part_expected: Kahane-Khintchine: for any sequence (a_n) in a Banach space and Rademacher epsilon_n: ||sum epsilon_n a_n||_{L^p(Omega; X)} ~ ||sum epsilon_n a_n||_{L^q(Omega; X)} for 1 <= p, q < infinity, with constants depending only on p, q (and the Banach space's type/cotype).
paired.multi_part_probe: For random series and Banach-space-valued integrals: (a) state Kahane-Khintchine; (b) define the type and cotype of a Banach space; (c) state which spaces have type 2 and which have cotype 2 (give a representative example each), and what this implies for vector-valued harmonic analysis.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Kahane-Khintchine: L^p-norms of sum epsilon_n a_n are equivalent for all 1 <= p < infinity (with constants depending on p, q, X); (b) X has type p (in [1, 2]) if E ||sum epsilon_n a_n||^p_X <= C^p sum ||a_n||^p_X; X has cotype q (in [2, infinity]) if E ||sum epsilon_n a_n||^q_X >= c^q sum ||a_n||^q_X; (c) Hilbert spaces have type 2 and cotype 2 (sharp); L^p has type min(p, 2) and cotype max(p, 2); type 2 implies UMD (with caveats) and is needed for vector-valued Mihlin/Hormander multiplier theorems and for L^p-vector-valued singular integrals to remain bounded.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is K-K; multi-part adds type/cotype + vector-valued harmonic analysis
paired.source: Khintchine 1923; Kahane 1985 'Some random series of functions' (Cambridge); Maurey-Pisier 1976 (Studia Mathematica)

paired.single_part_probe: State the De Giorgi-Nash-Moser theorem on Holder regularity of solutions to divergence-form elliptic equations with bounded measurable coefficients.
paired.single_part_expected: For a uniformly elliptic divergence-form operator L u = div(A(x) grad u) on a domain in R^n with A in L^infinity uniformly elliptic, every weak solution u in W^{1,2} is locally Holder continuous: u in C^{0, alpha}_{loc} for some alpha = alpha(n, ellipticity) in (0, 1).
paired.multi_part_probe: For divergence-form elliptic equations with bounded measurable coefficients on R^n: (a) state the De Giorgi-Nash-Moser theorem; (b) what is the role of the Moser iteration / Harnack inequality in the proof; (c) what fails for non-divergence-form equations L u = sum a_{ij}(x) partial_i partial_j u with bounded measurable a_{ij}, and what ellipticity refinement closes the gap?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) De Giorgi-Nash-Moser: weak solutions are locally Holder continuous, with alpha depending only on n and the ellipticity ratio; (b) Moser's iteration uses the De Giorgi level-set decay (each level set's measure decays geometrically) iteratively to get a sup-bound from an L^p-bound; combined with the Harnack inequality (positive solutions on a ball are comparable on a smaller ball), this yields oscillation decay and hence Holder continuity; (c) for non-divergence form with merely bounded measurable coefficients, regularity is OPEN in dimensions n >= 3 in general; the Krylov-Safonov 1979 theorem proves Harnack and C^{0, alpha} regularity for non-divergence form when the leading coefficients are bounded measurable AND uniformly elliptic, and is the analogue closing the non-divergence gap.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is regularity; multi-part adds Moser iteration + non-divergence gap
paired.source: De Giorgi 1957 (Memoirs Acad. Sci. Torino); Nash 1958 (Amer. J. Math.); Moser 1960 (CPAM); Krylov-Safonov 1979 (Math. USSR-Izv.)

paired.single_part_probe: State the prime number theorem in its simplest form.
paired.single_part_expected: pi(x) ~ x / log x as x -> infinity, where pi(x) counts primes <= x.
paired.multi_part_probe: For the distribution of primes: (a) state the prime number theorem; (b) describe how the PNT follows from non-vanishing of zeta(s) on the line Re(s) = 1; (c) what is the Riemann-von Mangoldt explicit formula relating pi(x) to the zeros of zeta?
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) pi(x) ~ x/log x or equivalently pi(x) ~ Li(x) := integral_2^x dt/log t; (b) the PNT is equivalent to the non-vanishing of zeta on Re(s) = 1: zeta(1 + it) != 0 for all real t; this uses the Hadamard / de la Vallee Poussin argument that 3 + 4 cos theta + cos 2 theta >= 0 to control |zeta(s)|^3 |zeta(s+it)|^4 |zeta(s+2it)| from below as s -> 1+ via Mertens-type identities; (c) Riemann-von Mangoldt explicit formula: psi(x) = x - sum_rho x^rho/rho - log(2 pi) - (1/2) log(1 - x^{-2}), where rho ranges over non-trivial zeta zeros and psi(x) = sum_{p^k <= x} log p; this exhibits PNT as the leading order x and quantifies the error term in terms of distance from the critical line.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is PNT; multi-part adds Re(s)=1 nonvanishing + explicit formula
paired.source: Hadamard 1896; de la Vallee Poussin 1896; Riemann 1859 (explicit formula); von Mangoldt 1895

paired.single_part_probe: State the Hardy uncertainty principle.
paired.single_part_expected: Hardy 1933: if both f and its Fourier transform hat{f} satisfy |f(x)| <= C e^{-pi a x^2} and |hat{f}(xi)| <= C e^{-pi b xi^2} with a b > 1, then f = 0; with a b = 1, f is a Gaussian (up to normalization).
paired.multi_part_probe: For the Hardy uncertainty principle on R: (a) state the theorem in the form involving the product of the Gaussian decay rates; (b) describe the proof technique (Phragmen-Lindelof on a strip via Fourier inversion); (c) state the corresponding multidimensional version on R^n.
paired.multi_part_expected: (a) Hardy uncertainty: |f(x)| <= e^{-pi a x^2} and |hat{f}(xi)| <= e^{-pi b xi^2} with a b > 1 force f = 0; with a b = 1 f is a Gaussian; (b) Apply Fourier inversion to f, write the resulting integral as a contour integral and apply Phragmen-Lindelof to e^{pi a x^2} f(x) which is bounded by 1 on R; the Fourier transform's exponential decay extends f to a holomorphic function in a strip and the bound implies it is at most a polynomial, then a Gaussian; (c) on R^n: |f(x)| <= e^{-pi a |x|^2} and |hat{f}(xi)| <= e^{-pi b |xi|^2} with a b > 1 implies f = 0; same proof technique applies dimension-by-dimension with Plancherel.
paired.decomposition_target: single-part is statement; multi-part adds proof technique + multidim
paired.source: Hardy 1933 (Journal of the London Mathematical Society)

open_case.description: Furstenberg set conjecture in R^2: for s in [0,1], dim_H of an (s, 1-s)-Furstenberg set (set containing an s-dim subset of a line in every direction) is at least 1 + s
open_case.result: Bourgain 2003 conditional bounds; Wolff 1999 partial bounds; Orponen-Shmerkin 2023 (paraphrase) major progress; sharp form OPEN; recent strong results in some s ranges
open_case.status: OPEN in full range

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Furstenberg set conjecture is settled by Marstrand or by Wolff
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; subtle distinction between projection theorem and Furstenberg sets

trivial_case.description: Sets of fractional dimension: rectifiability characterizations of curves of finite length
trivial_case.result: Riesz brothers theorem (1916): if Omega is a Jordan domain in C with rectifiable boundary, then harmonic measure is mutually absolutely continuous with arc-length measure

open_case.description: Higher-dim analog: characterization of rectifiable sets by harmonic measure absolute continuity in n >= 3
open_case.result: Azzam-Mourgoglou-Tolsa series 2015-2020: characterized in some cases (uniformly rectifiable Ahlfors regular boundaries); full case OPEN
open_case.status: OPEN in higher generality

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model attributes the higher-dim characterization to F. and M. Riesz
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; planar settled by classical names, higher-dim recent + open

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Beilinson-Bloch / regulator-period conjecture connections to L-values

trivial_case.description: Dirichlet class number formula: residue of L(s, chi) at s=1 expresses class numbers of corresponding number fields

open_case.description: Beilinson conjecture: special L-values L(s, M) at non-critical integers expressed via regulators of motivic cohomology
open_case.result: proven for specific cases (CM elliptic curves, products thereof, some Shimura varieties); general OPEN
open_case.status: OPEN in general

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model conflates Dirichlet's classical formula with the Beilinson conjecture's full generality
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; classical analytic-side known, motivic generalization open

trivial_vs_open.conjecture_family: Stable solutions of semilinear elliptic equations (De Giorgi conjecture)

trivial_case.description: De Giorgi conjecture for stable monotone solutions of -Delta u = u - u^3 (Allen-Cahn) on R^n: in n <= 3, level sets are hyperplanes
trivial_case.venue: Math. Ann. (Ghoussoub-Gui); J. Amer. Math. Soc. (Ambrosio-Cabre)

open_case.description: De Giorgi conjecture in n >= 4 (stable monotone solutions are 1D)
open_case.result: Savin 2009: n=4..8 proven under additional hypothesis; del Pino-Kowalczyk-Wei 2011 counterexample in n=9; full conjecture FALSE in n >= 9; SHARP version (n <= 8) holds under additional hypothesis
open_case.status: RESOLVED (false in n>=9; n=4..8 proven under extra hypothesis); sharper structural questions remain

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims De Giorgi conjecture is 'open in all dimensions n >= 4' or 'true in all dimensions' without distinguishing the resolution stage by stage
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; resolved in pieces with dimensional pieces having different status

trivial_case.description: Falconer distance set conjecture in R^2: if E in R^2 has dim_H E > 1, then the distance set Delta(E) := {|x-y| : x,y in E} has positive Lebesgue measure
trivial_case.result: Conjecturally yes if dim_H > 1; dim_H > 1 is necessary (counterexample at exactly dim 1)
trivial_case.proven_by: various: Wolff 1999 dim_H > 4/3; Guth-Iosevich-Ou-Wang 2020 (paraphrase) dim_H > 5/4 in 2D; sharp threshold 1 still open
trivial_case.status: OPEN at sharp threshold 1 in 2D; partial bounds proven

open_case.description: Falconer conjecture in R^n, n >= 2 at sharp threshold dim_H E > n/2
open_case.result: Erdogan 2005 dim_H > n/2 + 1/3 (or similar partial bound); sharp threshold n/2 OPEN in all n >= 2
open_case.status: OPEN at sharp threshold

trivial_vs_open.common_failure_pattern: FM-08: model claims Falconer distance set is 'proven by Wolff' or attributes the latest 5/4 bound to Falconer himself
trivial_vs_open.doctrine_relevance: FM-08; conjecture name, partial bounds, sharp threshold all distinct
